data.title: SunScout Holding Targets IPO Despite High Valuation And Risks
data.description:
SunScout Is Seeking An IPO For A Business Shift

SunScout Holding Limited (SNSC) has filed to raise $22 million in an IPO of its Class A ordinary shares, according to an F-1 registration statement.

The company develops solar-powered products for various applications.

SNSC wants to pursue the autonomous mower market but has numerous execution & growth risks, and its valuation expectations at IPO are excessive.

My outlook on the SNSC IPO is to sell (avoid it)

What Does SunScout Do?

New Zealand-based SunScout Holding has developed solar-powered robotic mowers and other products and solar services that it is commercializing in commercial and consumer markets.

Most of its revenue has come from its solar power development solutions and engineering products and services, but management says the future growth driver of the company is going to be from its SunScout Products offerings:

  • Solar-powered robotic mowers - approaching market-ready

  • Solar shelter - in development

  • Go-Easy Campervan and EV Utility Van - in development

A pie chart showing the company's revenue contribution for the second half of calendar 2025 is shown here:

SunScout has distribution potential for its autonomous mowing product line in Germany, Australia, and New Zealand.

The company's mower product line is divided into three types:

  • Eco - residential

  • Pro - light commercial

  • ProMax - large-scale institutional

The firm is led by Chairman and Chief Executive Officer Mr. Edwin Cywinski, who has extensive experience in the commercial solar industry. His son, Marc Cywinski, is Chief Operations Officer.

SunScout has received $110,234 in fair market value investment from AE Equity Limited, Solerin Equity Limited, and Mr. Jacob Pretorius, among others.

What is SunScout’s Primary Market?

SunScout is positioning its future growth path as reliant on its Products group, so it may be evaluated based on the robotic mower market, which was an estimated $9.3 billion in 2025, according to a 2026 Grand View Research market research report.

The market is forecast to reach nearly $22 billion by 2033, which would represent a CAGR of 11.4% from 2026 to 2033.

If achieved, that growth would be a reasonably robust rate of growth.

The North American market accounted for the highest share by revenue, with 38.2% in 2025.

The chart below shows the historical and projected future growth path of the robotic lawn mowers' market worldwide through 2033 by battery capacity:

Competitors in the autonomous mower market include:

  • Husqvarna Group (HUSQF) - large incumbent

  • Segway Navimow - premium residential

  • Mammotion - innovator, large residential lawns

  • WORX - incumbent

  • Greenworks - innovator

  • Ecovacs - challenger from the vacuum sector

  • Sunseeker - Value segment

  • Robomow - smaller incumbent

  • Ambrogio - premium, strong in Europe

  • FireFly Automatix - new entrant

The primary growth drivers of the autonomous, battery-powered mower market will be the desire to automate landscaping work, a trend toward electrification of outdoor equipment, and demand for lower-emission, lower-noise lawn maintenance.

The company's strengths include its three-tiered product line, residential and commercial markets, and a multi-channel go-to-market strategy.

Weaknesses for SunScout are its early stage of primary market commercialization, small sales infrastructure, and development stage for distribution relationships.

The firm’s opportunities are primarily from a growing autonomous mower market and expansion into Europe and the U.S.

Threats to its prospects are improving technologies from incumbents and new entrants, distribution delays, and product reliability and safety requirements.

SunScout’s Recent Financial Results

The company has produced most of its revenue from its other business lines, which are partially funding its efforts in the mower industry.

Topline revenue growth of 17.1% for the first half of fiscal 2026 vs. the prior year's period was due to growth in its legacy business segments, i.e., its Solar Power Development Solutions and Engineered Products & Services, rather than growth from its future focus of SunScout Products (Mowers, etc).

SunScout’s IPO Plan

SNSC plans to sell four million Class A ordinary shares at a proposed midpoint price of $5.50 per share, for gross proceeds of $22 million, not including underwriter allotments.

Class A shareholders will receive one vote per share, and each Class B share will be entitled to twenty (20) votes per share.

Shareholder sale lock-ups will be for the typical 180-day period after the effective date of the prospectus.

However, the company may sell additional shares after only three months from the effective date, an unusual term that is not often seen in IPOs.

This means management might be planning to issue a follow-on offering relatively soon after the IPO, potentially causing greater share supply to come to market in a short period of time.

Management intends to use the net proceeds from the IPO as described here:

We currently intend to use the net proceeds from this offering for the following purposes: establish a manufacturing plan, marketing and promotion campaigns, product development, inventory, repayment of loan, payment under the Brightway MPA and working capital.

(Source: SEC)

The firm’s online IPO roadshow presentation is not currently available.

SunScout said it is not aware of any legal claims or proceedings that would have a material adverse effect on its financial condition or business.

Underwriters of the IPO are Dominari Securities and Revere Securities.

Capital And Liquidity Dynamics

As of December 31, 2025, SunScout had $893,278 in cash and equivalents and total liabilities of $3.5 million, of which approximately $2.4 million was in debt.

Free cash flow for the trailing twelve months ended December 31, 2025, was $698,677 after capital expenditures of $134,522, a modest capex intensity.

The company's MBIE debt was meaningful because it has a fixed interest rate of 11.84%, although the IPO’s proceeds will presumably be used to pay it off.

SunScout has a current ratio of almost 3x, which is healthy.

SunScout Is Seeking Expansion Capital Ahead Of Transition

SunScout wants public investors to fund its transition away from lower-potential business services to manufacturing autonomous, electric-powered mowing equipment.

The firm is still at the stage of finalizing its production plans, so it faces numerous execution risks in the coming quarters.

Also, SunScout’s historically high margins from its legacy service offerings will likely be cut in half once it begins production of its mower lines.

The company’s growth strategy involves establishing a U.S. manufacturing footprint, growing international operating complexity for this small New Zealand-based company.

SNSC also wants to follow a distributor-led international expansion model, which shows significant reliance on third parties to produce sales growth.

Management also intends to expend resources on direct-to-consumer via online sales, which can be expensive and take some time to dial in for positive ROI.

The overall market for autonomous mowers is expected to grow at a moderately robust rate of growth in the coming years, so the firm has positive industry growth dynamics in its favor.

But its growth plans require many things to go right, in the right sequence, and the firm will be dependent on distributors.

Related party transactions have been substantial and include a $5 million acquisition of the owner’s Brightway entity, more fully detailed here.

As to valuation, I’ve compared SunScout to the Husqvarna Group (HUSQF) for major metrics shown in the table below:

While Husqvarna is a much larger, incumbent firm, the valuation variances that SunScout is proposing at IPO are extreme.

My overall summary of the SunScout IPO is:

  • Positives - Focus on a growing mower market, FCF positive on a small base, post-IPO liquidity

  • Uncertain - Numerous execution risks, distributor sales reliance

  • Negatives - Excessive valuation, $5 million due owners for Brightway MPA

Given the company's numerous execution risks and excessive valuation assumptions, my outlook on the SNSC IPO is to sell (avoid it).

data.title: Capital Clean Energy Carriers: Stable LNG Cash Flows With A Rerating Catalyst
data.description:
Having completed a transition from container shipping to LNG and gas, Capital Clean Energy Carriers Corp. (CCEC) is a vastly different company from what it was a few years ago. They now mostly have multi-year revenue contracts. That implies stable cash flows but limited upside due to high capital needs; no surprise that the market values Capital Clean Energy around 10x earnings.

However, their recent joint venture deal could change all that. Right now, this is a single-ship JV, but if they can scale that, it could become a new economic model that is less capital-intensive and therefore deserving of higher multiples.

So we get an unusual setup. Purely by current fundamentals, the stock looks fairly valued and should be rated a Hold. However, with this new structural shift that the market doesn’t appear to be pricing in, there’s a new dynamic here. Downside is supported by contracted revenues; however, upside comes from multiple expansions based on the new JV deal(s). Therefore, I rate Capital Clean Energy a cautious Buy—not for its yield, nor even for its growth, but for potential rerating due to this transition.

What Has Changed

Capital Clean Energy is now a gas-focused shipping platform, having sold 14 container vessels since late 2023 (only one to go). Currently they have 12 LNG carriers in the water and 9 on order, with total committed CapEx of roughly $2.4 billion. They also have their first LCO2/multi-gas carrier. So their pivot to LNG/gas is complete.

We can see this reflected in their revenue profile. They now have ~$2.9 billion of contracted LNG revenues with an average duration of 6.9 years and optionally another $1.4 billion and 4 more years. This helps understand the current valuation of 10x earnings, based on stable and visible earnings, but not a growth story. For that growth story, we have the new JV structure.

A recent JV transaction marks a very interesting new phase. Capital Clean Energy is selling the LNG carrier Amore Mio I (a 2023-built, 174,000 cbm vessel) into a joint venture where it has 51% control with a partner from the BGN Group. The vessel is being valued at $230M, plus the JV has already secured a 10-year charter with another 6 years optional. The potential value of the charter is ~$485.6 million in total revenue through 2043.

On the surface, this looks like a standard partial asset sale. In reality, it is doing several things at once:

This structure lets Capital Clean Energy monetize assets and recycle capital under an extended contract without materially reducing its economic exposure. And this could be a template.

If Capital Clean Energy can repeat this structure for more ships, they could continue to lower equity per asset and improve return on capital, basically multiplying the entire structural benefits just outlined. That would make them basically a capital-recycling platform for contracted LNG vessels—a much different business from a traditional, balance sheet-heavy shipping operator.

This isn’t something the market seems to be pricing Capital Clean Energy in for.

Earnings Profile

We can see this difference between current valuation and growth potential from the JV in the earnings.

Capital Clean Energy reported quarterly revenue of ~$98 million, down ~6% YoY, and net income was ~$28 million. Flat revenues, modest growth—that's the unremarkable picture seen on the surface, and the reason why the stock trades as it does.

However, looking closely, it becomes clear that depreciation and interest expense together come to ~$45.8 million, or ~47% of quarterly revenue. That means operating performance as seen through reported net income is heavily distorted by Capital Clean Energy’s financing structure. In other words, the business may be generating stable charter income, but equity earnings are held back by the capital-intensive nature of the business. The same thing is seen in cash flow. Despite generating $232.8 million in operating cash flow in FY2025, most of it doesn’t flow to equity because of the huge capex requirement.

The JV structure becomes relevant right here. It basically gives wings to a balance-sheet-heavy business. It could enable Capital Clean Energy to decouple growth from capital intensity and perhaps cause the market to re-rate the stock.

Dividend And Balance Sheet

The high capital requirement limits the amount of cash that actually reaches shareholders. Capital Clean Energy has been paying quarterly dividends for 75 quarters. Currently it pays $0.15, or $0.60 annually, at roughly 3% yield. The payout ratio is low at ~30% of earnings. This supports sustainability but also shows that most cash is still going to capex and not getting back to shareholders. Thus, at just 3%, the dividend is working only as a support and doesn’t form a core part of the investment thesis. It is nice to have but doesn’t compensate for the capital intensity and doesn’t drive total returns.

Moving on, the balance sheet is still the key limitation. Debt is $2.3–$2.4 billion against ~$1.1–1.2 billion in equity, so leverage is significant, and servicing that debt holds back earnings. This limits equity upside, so they need to be disciplined in their capital allocation, especially where they are working through that huge $2+ billion new build CapEx cycle. This is exactly why the market continues to be conservative with its multiples. Until capital intensity improves, valuation expansion isn’t going to come from fundamentals alone, but from other things—for example, through more JVs kicking in.

Valuation

Like I noted already, Capital Clean Energy is now valued at roughly ~10x forward earnings, with an EPS of around $2. This is consistent with how the business is assessed today: a stable, contract-backed, but capital-heavy shipper with limited growth potential. If you take that angle, the stock looks fairly valued. But there’s rerating potential if the JV thing takes off. I have modeled these scenarios below:

Scenario Analysis

The base case here is where we are today—visible earnings, capped upside—translating into mid-single-digit returns, which includes the 3% dividend.

The bear case is about 25% below today’s level. It requires a weakening LNG cycle or balance sheet pressure. That could justify lower multiples, especially given the capital-heavy business and high borrowing from external sources.

The bull case forms my thesis. Earnings growth is minimal, as you can see, but there’s a somewhat higher multiple if the JV model can scale. If that happens, Capital Clean Energy should be able to reduce equity per asset and improve returns on capital. This could lead to a rerating, because then it becomes a different business model altogether. Even a partial shift towards an asset-light model can support a rerating toward 12–13x earnings.

The takeaway here is that while Capital Clean Energy looks fairly valued as it is now, the JV development says the investment case is not so static. On the one side, the downside is blocked by long-term contracted revenue, and on the other, the upside now has a narrative: Capital Clean Energy can scale these capital-recycling JVs. Even if earnings take time to grow, given the contracted nature, the new model can change the return profile in how earnings are perceived and valued.

That asymmetry—limited fundamental downside but credible rerating potential—is what supports a cautious Buy.

Conclusion

Capital Clean Energy today is in an interesting position. Its transition to LNG is complete, and it now operates with visible contracted cash flows. This supports the downside and justifies the current valuation. However, those same characteristics (along with capital intensity) limit upside. This is why Capital Clean Energy remains very range-bound, as seen in its 52-week chart.

The new JV structure could change that equation if it can be scaled up. In future quarters, this is something we will be looking for. If it scales, the business becomes a higher-return, less capital-heavy structure.

Current earnings do not reflect this shift, so current market valuation doesn’t reflect this either. But since the downside is supported already, this growth strategy is a credible pathway for multiple expansions and presents an inherent asymmetry in the stock.

Therefore, I rate Capital Clean Energy a cautious Buy—not for the yield or near-term growth, but for a change in market perception if there is such a structural transition.

data.title: Shopify Preview: Expensive, But Not Overhyped Ahead Of Q1
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I think Shopify (SHOP) is an interesting story in software and e-commerce. I also think that Shopify’s price has declined with much of the rest of the internet/software space. However, I believe the market is being overly concerned about near-term volatility and less concerned about the underlying strength of Shopify’s growth.

My investment thesis is that Shopify will become increasingly important as one of the fundamental platforms for what I call “new” digital commerce, particularly as a result of the growth of “agentic commerce.” Orders generated by AI-powered searches have grown significantly since 2025, and as you’ll see later on in this article, Shopify has entered into an agreement with Google regarding the development of a “Universal Commerce Protocol,” which will make it even easier for customers to complete checkout and pay for their products via AI. For me, this is one of the most underappreciated aspects of the story.

Shopify has continued to post high revenue growth rates. Currently over 30%, combined with new cohorts of higher productivity merchants and increased penetration within the enterprise segment. With respect to margins, I am confident that investors remain too conservative in their expectations. Operating expenses already came in below guidance in Q4, and the potential for Shopify to implement artificial intelligence internally is likely to keep costs lower than they were prior to doing so.

While valuation is certainly elevated in absolute terms, Shopify is still trading below some of its historical valuation averages. Furthermore, as mentioned earlier, I believe that Shopify’s fundamentals are significantly improved compared to where they have historically been.

Therefore, despite being expensive, I remain bullish on Shopify. While the company is not inexpensive, I firmly believe that it deserves a premium multiple if the company can deliver strong Q1 results and provide constructive Q2 guidance (and demonstrate initial success with its proposed “AI Commerce Cycle”).

Previous Quarter: A Strong Finish, With One Clear Question Mark

Shopify had a great fourth quarter in 2025. Gross Merchandise Volume (GMV) grew 31% YOY (year-over-year), as did revenues. To me, what matters isn't how well Shopify exceeded its earnings estimates. What matters is Shopify is continuing to grow so rapidly.

In terms of the growth areas of Shopify's businesses, "Merchant Solutions" grew about 35% from last year, while "Subscription Solutions" were up approximately 17%. GMV totalled almost $123.8 billion. Revenues came in at approximately $3.7 billion. Non-GAAP Operating Income was $760M with a non-GAAP operating margin greater than 20%. This is an area where many investors continue to be underestimating the magnitude of Shopify's operational leverage. Shopify is transitioning from being strictly a growth stock into a growth stock with real operational leverage.

Guidance for Q1 '26 was also quite positive. Management guided for Q1 revenue growth in the low-30% range and gross profit growth in the high-20% range, with free cash flow margins expected to remain in the low-to-mid teens. Shopify also announced a $2 Billion Buyback Program. I see this announcement as important. It appears to me that Shopify has sufficient cash generation to begin returning capital to shareholders via buybacks while maintaining their aggressive investments in Product, Payments, Enterprise and AI.

I also liked seeing improvement in new merchant cohorts. New merchants added in both '24 and '25 are generating GMV and revenue at a faster rate than previous merchant cohorts. While this may not seem like a big deal, I find this to be an extremely positive sign because it implies that Shopify is not simply adding more customers but is adding better customers.

My largest concern going forward remains margin mix. Merchant Solutions gross margin has come under pressure due to an increasing amount of volume moving through payments, which carries significantly lower gross margins than many other legacy revenue streams. I consider this debate to be valid. The business is certainly becoming more payment-heavy, which will negatively impact reported gross margin. However, I would rather own a company that is taking a larger percentage of transaction flows, even if it comes with a lower gross margin, than own a company that defends a higher gross margin profile but has less strategic relevance.

Upcoming Quarter Preview: Q1 2026

Q1 2026 is going to be all about one simple issue: "Is Shopify still getting bigger, or are we entering into a normalizing phase?"

My expectation for Q1 is a good, solid quarter again, GMV growth in the low-to-mid 30% range, and revenue growth around the low 30%. I believe this would be consistent with what has been provided by Shopify regarding their own guidance and with the evidence that Shopify continues to gain share in the e-commerce space. My expectation of the company's performance is neither too high nor too low. However, I expect the performance to exceed expectations.

I'm looking at 4 areas for confirmation on whether Shopify delivered a good quarter.

  1. First, I'm looking at GMV. If Shopify reports GMV greater than approximately $100 billion for the quarter, it will be a positive indicator that the platform is continuing to compound at a rapid pace. Second, I'd like to know how much was generated through North American activity compared to international activity. How much came from smaller merchants (SMB) vs. larger merchants (enterprise)? Finally, I'm interested in seeing how much of the growth came from larger merchants, particularly globally known brands. To me, the importance of the larger merchant story comes from its knack to influence investor sentiment and ultimately influence the valuation multiple assigned to Shopify. A platform that only uses small merchants will have a very different valuation model compared to a platform being used by global brands.
  2. Shopify is also evolving in terms of becoming more embedded within the transaction. Therefore, the second area I'd like to highlight is payments. Shop Pay, GPV penetration, and Merchant Solutions take rate are all significant indicators because they show where Shopify is headed. As Shopify evolves toward becoming more integrated into the transaction process, the opportunity to generate additional revenue increases. However, when you're generating revenue from transactions, there will be some impact on your gross margin due to lower gross margins associated with payment processing. I don't mind absorbing some gross margin pressure if it means Shopify captures a larger portion of the commerce stack.
  3. The third area I'd like to highlight is profitability. While I understand the desire to see dramatic margin improvement, I don't feel it's necessary to see that type of growth this quarter. What I do hope to see is continued aggressive investment spending if Shopify believes it has an attractive growth opportunity ahead of it. Additionally, I want to see that Shopify demonstrates operating discipline. If operational costs continue to grow at a slow rate relative to revenue growth and if AI tools continue to help improve internal efficiency, then perhaps investors will begin to realize that Shopify's free cash flow profile is more robust than previously thought.
  4. Finally, I'd like to highlight agentic commerce as an area that could provide the most unexpected pleasant surprises. Investors are currently divided as to whether AI commerce represents a meaningful opportunity for monetization or simply another layer of software. I think that Shopify will have an opportunity to make a greater amount of money from Agentic Commerce than most other companies do. This is because checking out, paying (for example, credit card processing), fraud prevention, collecting taxes, etc., are generally much harder to accomplish. If Shopify can provide specific numbers regarding how well their merchants are adopting this technology and what additional revenue streams may exist through agentic commerce, then there should be a very positive reaction in terms of the stock price.

Positive Reaction

Reasons for a rise in Shopify's stock price include the reporting of the following from Shopify:

  1. GMV greater than or equal to $100 billion during Q1;
  2. Q2 revenue guidance consistent with growth of approximately 30%+ per year;
  3. and, an affirmation by management that they are confident in their ability to maintain margins.

The thesis would be strengthened by concrete progress in agentic commerce adoption and monetization.

Negative Reaction

Reasons for a negative reaction in Shopify’s stock include:

  1. GMV growth below 30%
  2. poor Q2 revenue guidance;
  3. higher-than-anticipated margin compression;
  4. and/or ambiguity regarding monetization opportunities related to AI capabilities.

For now, I consider Shopify to be a "high-expectations" stock, but not overly hyped. All that needs to happen is for Shopify to demonstrate execution. If it does, I think investors may be forced to rethink their decelerating narrative.

Valuation Assessment

Shopify isn't cheap. This is simple and true.

With a price-to-forward earnings ratio (P/E) of 68x and Enterprise Value/Sales ratio of 13.8x, this stock clearly represents a premium to nearly all of the companies listed in the table. At 29.2x, Amazon's forward P/E ratio is less than half of Shopify's. Also significantly cheaper are Salesforce, at 13.5x; Adobe, at 10.2x; and MercadoLibre, with a lower Enterprise Value/Sales multiple, despite having much stronger sales growth.

Therefore, the better question is: "Is the premium worth it?"

In my opinion, the premium is worth paying, provided Shopify continues to report annualized sales growth of greater than 25-30%, along with significant improvement in free cash flow conversion. At present, Shopify is reporting 30% sales growth, while the other names mentioned here (Amazon (12.4%), Salesforce (9.6%), and Adobe (11.0%)) have reported much slower sales growth.

A company that generates such strong sales growth should deserve a premium. However, the key is that Shopify is not generating profits anywhere near those of the more mature software names. For example, Shopify's Free Cash Flow Margin is 17.4% versus 34.7% for Salesforce and 42.2% for Adobe.

This is why I do not believe you can value Shopify exactly like one values a mature software name right now. To receive a high enough valuation based upon free cash flow generated by Shopify, there must be evidence that the current rapid sales growth will eventually generate a large amount of free cash flow over time.

The most difficult comparable company for me was MercadoLibre because it is growing faster, has a higher FCF margin, and trades at a lower EV/Sales multiple. However, I do think Shopify deserves a small premium for the fact that it has greater exposure to developed markets, a clean platform model, and tremendous potential for long-term upside from AI-driven commerce.

Under the upside case, if Shopify successfully demonstrates that AI commerce can grow gross merchandise volume (GMV), increase take rates, and ultimately create new revenue opportunities, then the current multiple does not appear so extreme after all.

Risks to the Buy Thesis

The first risk is that agentic commerce takes longer to monetize than I expect. This is the most exciting part of the Shopify story, but it is also the least proven one. I can believe in AI-driven shopping, AI search, automated checkout, and new commerce flows, but investors still need evidence that this becomes real GMV and real revenue for Shopify. If management only provides general comments about AI-driven orders, merchant adoption, usage of the Universal Commerce Protocol (UCP), or future pricing, the market may begin to perceive agentic commerce as a nice narrative rather than a financial driver. At which point the multiple could contract rapidly.

My second concern is gross margin pressure from payments. Shopify is increasingly embedded in the transaction layer, and strategically speaking, I like that. However, generally speaking, payments have lower gross margins than pure software revenue. If merchant solutions growth continues to be stronger, yet the gross margin continues to decline, investors will begin to question how much operating leverage can Shopify really deliver. I would be willing to accept some gross margin compression if Shopify captures more of the commerce stack; however, there is an upper limit. If gross margin erosion accelerates rapidly, defending the valuation becomes significantly more difficult.

The third risk I have identified is "enterprise execution". This is central to my thesis as it relates to Shopify's strategy of going "up-market" with larger merchants (as they generate more Gross Merchandise Volume ("GMV"), greater payment volume, and provide for longer-term relevance on the platform). However, big brand enterprise sales cycles are generally much longer and more complicated than those for small-to-medium-sized businesses ("SMB"). Brands don't migrate overnight. Enterprise customers require integration, customization, support, and, in many cases, different pricing. Therefore, if Shopify wins logos but takes too long to monetize them into real revenue, we may see GMV growth slowing down at a rate that is faster than what has been anticipated. For me, this represents manageable risk; however, it does represent relevant risk.

Conclusion

For me, Shopify is an interesting purchase opportunity today. My rating is still "buy." In terms of multiples, the stock is not cheap, and I would not pretend otherwise. However, if the upcoming Q1 results confirm GMV to be at least $100 billion, and revenue growth of at least 30%, and they're keeping their operating expense discipline intact, my expectations for SHOP's future price target are around USD 180 within the next year.

It's essential to note that I'm basing this expectation almost entirely on how well Q1 comes out. While it doesn't have to be the "perfect" quarterly report, there has to be some kind of proof that the company still has a growing business model and that SHOP is more than an expensive software stock with a compelling storyline.

data.title: 3 Takeaways From Kevin Warsh's Fed Chair Hearings
data.description:
By Brian Levitt, Chief Global Market Strategist and Head of Strategy & Insights

This week, I’m going to take a brief break from talking about the conflict with Iran. Not because it lacks importance, but because it appears to be of fleeting interest to financial markets. Stocks hit all-time highs midweek and closed the week near those levels.1 Bond yields have generally been range-bound.2 Credit spreads have barely moved.3 The US dollar has generally weakened through April.4 In short, markets appeared to believe that the worst of the conflict is behind us and that conditions will improve over time.

At the same time, the economic data demonstrated resilience. Global manufacturing Purchasing Managers’ Index (PMI) readings, in the US and UK, remain strong and consistent with expansion.5 US retail sales surprised to the upside,6 reinforcing the view that the consumer remains engaged despite elevated gasoline prices.7 If markets are a forward-looking discounting mechanism, they appear to be telling us that geopolitics is no longer the dominant variable and that the economic backdrop remains workable.

Instead, I want to focus this week on something that I believe will matter far more to the US economy and markets over the medium to longer term. That’s the leadership transition at the Federal Reserve (Fed). The fact that Kevin Warsh is emerging as the next chairman is noteworthy on several levels. Here are my three key takeaways from his testimony to Congress last week.

1. Importance of Fed independence

There’s concern about Fed independence and the fear that Warsh may be placed at the Fed to do the president’s bidding. In his testimony, Warsh emphasized the importance of Fed independence and suggested an intention to navigate policy choices without unnecessarily provoking the administration.8

Whether that balance can be maintained over time remains an open question, but for now, investors appeared to be willing to give him the benefit of the doubt. Longer-term US inflation expectations remain well-contained,9 suggesting that markets aren’t currently pricing in concerns about political interference in monetary policy.

2. More nuanced interpretation of inflation

Warsh’s tone is increasingly dovish compared to his first go-around at the Fed as a member of the Board of Governors. Back then, he was (inexplicably?) more hawkish in the aftermath of the Global Financial Crisis. What stood out most to me during last week’s hearings was his openness to a more nuanced interpretation of inflation, especially in an environment shaped by artificial intelligence (AI)-driven productivity gains, tariffs, and oil-related shocks.

Warsh’s support of inflation measures such as median inflation, which focuses on the middle observation, and trimmed mean inflation, which excludes extreme moves on both ends, may look to some like moving the goalposts. But in the current environment, they likely make more sense, in my view. The Fed shouldn’t be setting policy based on temporary price spikes driven by tariffs, war, or supply disruptions. These alternative measures are explicitly designed to look through those shocks and better capture underlying inflation trends.

In practice, this may not represent a dramatic break from how Chair Powell approached tariffs and geopolitical risks, but Warsh appeared more willing to formalize that framework by broadening the set of inflation indicators policymakers emphasize.

3. Reducing the size of the Fed’s balance sheet - slowly

Balance sheet policy is the area where he sounded more hawkish at first, but there’s an important nuance. Warsh signaled an interest in reducing the size of the Fed’s balance sheet, while being explicit that it isn’t something that can or should happen quickly. He acknowledged that it took decades to build the balance sheet and that unwinding it will require time, patience, and care.

Just as important, any changes to balance sheet policy will require agreement across the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC), reinforcing that this would be a deliberate and collective process rather than a unilateral shift in policy.

Bottom line

The upshot is that what I heard was broadly dovish, pragmatic, and respectful of institutional independence. Markets generally seemed to agree. I view this combination as supportive of stocks, and it reinforces my longstanding inclination not to fight the Fed.

data.title: Intel: A Very Strong Q1 Report, But Remains A 'Hold'
data.description:
Last week, we received first-quarter results from Intel (INTC). The chip giant is in the midst of a major turnaround, and that process is finally nearing its latter stages. The company announced huge top- and bottom-line beats for Q1 while giving tremendous guidance for the current quarter. The only problem now continues to be a very lofty valuation, so I would suggest waiting for the next pullback before jumping in.

Previous coverage of the name

It's been about three months since I last covered Intel, with my latest article coming after the company's fourth-quarter results. While the company announced solid top and bottom line beats for the December period, guidance for the March period was a bit disappointing. Investors shrugged off that bad news, however, deciding to focus on a brighter future, so the stock has surged almost 88% since, compared to a less than 3% rally in the S&P 500.

A look at the Q1 results

For the March period, Intel reported revenues of $13.6 billion. This number was up 7% over the prior year period, handily beating street estimates that called for a nearly 2% decline to $12.43 billion. This was an 11 percentage point swing in the top line change from Q4 2025, which had seen a 4% decrease. Intel products, which were down 1% in Q4, showed a 9% increase in Q1 2026, fueled by the all-important Data Center and AI segment that went from up 9% to up 22%. Client Computing also saw an 8 percentage point improvement to 1% growth. Intel Foundry went from up 4% to up 16% as well.

As revenues stabilized sequentially, Intel also saw nice margin growth. Non-GAAP gross margins came in at 41.0%, up 1.8 points year over year and 3.1 points sequentially. Even with the mid- to high single-digit percentage rise in revenues, main operating expenses were down roughly 9%. This led the company's adjusted operating margin to jump from 5.4% to 12.3%. This resulted in non-GAAP EPS of 29 cents per share, smashing the street that was looking for just one penny per share of adjusted profit.

Perhaps even more importantly, Intel showed that this wasn't just a one-quarter beat. Q2 guidance called for revenues of $13.8 billion to $14.8 billion, well above the street's average June quarter estimate of $13.06 billion. Non-GAAP margins are expected to decline a little sequentially but show massive improvement from Q2 2025. On the bottom line, management guided to adjusted earnings per share of 20 cents, which was 12 cents ahead of the street.

With a large Q1 beat and impressive Q2 guidance, we've finally seen analyst estimates start to really rise again. The current non-GAAP EPS estimate average stands at $1.08, nearly double the 55 cents it was at going into last week's report. The street now sees revenue growth this year of nearly 10.6%, tremendously above the 2.8% it was looking for before the Q1 report. As a result, Intel shares rocketed higher by 23.6% on Friday.

Balance sheet and cash flow update

In most years, Q1 is the seasonally weakest period for Intel revenues. That usually translates to the bottom line and eventually into cash flow. In recent years, Intel has been burning a lot of cash, and that's been especially true in the first quarter of the year. The chart below shows where Intel's net cash position, including equity investments, now stands.

Intel reported adjusted cash burn of a little more than $2 billion in the March quarter, but that was quite a bit better than the nearly $3.7 billion burned in Q1 2025. Operating cash flow was up more than $280 million; the company did spend over $1.2 billion less on gross capital expenditures, and it received about a billion dollars more in partner contributions. However, payments on finance leases were up over $200 million, and government incentives declined by over $700 million.

The company did use almost $600 million on acquisitions, so its net cash position worsened by a little more than $3 billion in Q1. Intel has about $32.8 billion in cash along with $8.5 billion in equity investments, against total debt of $45 billion. If we exclude the investments balance, the net cash position stands at a negative $12.2 billion. The balance sheet is still a lot healthier now than it was previously thanks to investments from the likes of Nvidia (NVDA), the US government, and others, although that did result in quite a bit of dilution last year. Cash flow trends should be better during the remainder of 2026 as the business improves.

The current valuation picture

Because Intel has limited adjusted profitability currently, the stock looks really expensive. Throw in the surge over the past few months, even with recent estimate hikes, and the numbers are quite high. In the table below, I've compared Intel with Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) and Nvidia in terms of expected revenue and earnings growth for this year and next. The P/E numbers are based on the respective year's earnings estimates, and as a reminder, Nvidia's 2026 fiscal year ends in January 2027.

Intel is projected to have the weakest revenue growth over the two years, although part of that is due to the Altera stake sale. The only reason why its 2026 adjusted earnings growth is so high is because of such a low prior year base. As for analyst ratings, nearly two-thirds of them had a "Hold" as of Sunday, with a few more "Buy" ratings than "Sell" ratings at this point. The average price target has more than tripled since early September, but at $74.89 currently, it represents more than 9% downside from Friday's close.

Final thoughts and recommendation

Intel reported tremendous results last week, sending shares to a new multi-year high. Not only did the company handily beat Q1 estimates on the top and bottom lines, but it also gave stronger-than-expected guidance for the current quarter. It looks like the turnaround is just about complete, and we should be seeing much better results moving forward. The only issue here remains a high valuation compared to peers that are expected to see much higher revenue growth rates.

While I think Intel is set up for a solid future ahead of it, I'm maintaining my "Hold" rating on the stock currently. This is purely a valuation call, especially given the large run we've seen in recent quarters. The worst days seem to be completely in the rearview mirror, but for those looking to buy, perhaps wait for the next pullback and maybe see if you can get shares below the average street price target.

data.title: Domino's Pizza: I'll Take Advantage Of Market Scares For A Slice Of Gains
data.description:
In the midst of the rising prices and stiff competition, many fast-food chain restaurants face higher cost pressures, weaker pricing power, and narrower margins. Yet, some players are still unmatched with their effective capitalization on technologies, strategic expansion, strong delivery arm, and solid brand recognition. Domino's Pizza, Inc. (DPZ) becomes even more of a buy stock as the selloff opens new buying opportunities. This is supported by its robust performance and fundamentals. Its valuation and technicals are also in sync and show upside potential.

Domino's Pizza Q1 2026: Strength Amid Market Scares

The year opened with lots of uncertainty as inflation remained stubborn despite the efforts of the Fed to stabilize it. Weaker purchasing power and tight competition continue to disrupt growth in many fast-food restaurants. Even pizza chains like Domino's Pizza, Inc., are no exceptions, as they continue to experience the mounting impact of macroeconomic headwinds. Yet, its strong opening of the year is a testament to its strategic restaurant management, resilient demand, and unwavering customer loyalty.

In Q1 2026, its operating revenue amounted to $1.15B, up by 3.5% YoY from $1.11B. This YoY growth was already weaker than in my previous coverage at 6.2% YoY, which suggests that inflation is starting to catch up with its growth. If we look at its revenue per segment, the slowing growth was solely due to its company-owned restaurants. Even so, we cannot just conclude that its performance has already started to weaken. After all, the number of its company-owned restaurants decreased to 262 versus 294 YoY. What's more interesting is that its global sales continued to increase, which showed sustained pricing and demand growth. We can measure it better if we look at it per restaurant. The average sales per restaurant reached $212,333 versus $209,022 YoY.

Systemwide Sales Restaurant Count Average Sales Per Restaurant
Q1 2025 $4,464,300,000 21,358 $209,022
Q1 2026 $4,739,700,000 22,322 $212,333

Likewise, its operating costs and expenses increased in line with its increasing restaurant capacity. However, you can see some improvements in efficiency. As you can see, the operating costs comprised 59.6% of the total revenue versus 60.2% YoY. This meant lower cost sensitivity, which was impressive considering the rising prices. Meanwhile, the G&A expenses comprised 9.7% of the total revenue versus 9.8% YoY. As a result, DPZ became more profitable, with an operating margin of 20.0% versus 18.8% YoY. Even if we exclude the impact of the sale of its owned restaurants, the operating margin will still be higher YoY at 19.3%.

Challenges

As a restaurant in the fast-food segment, DPZ is subject to risks associated with macroeconomic volatility. It may be more intense today because inflation accelerated once again to 3.3%. This shows that the Fed is still having a hard time keeping it under control. This also shows how domestic prices are easily affected by global market volatility. Note that tariff wars continue, while oil prices have skyrocketed recently. If these trends continue, the overall price level may be affected. Why? Higher oil prices mean more expensive fuel. This not only affects freight, industrial, and shipping but also the industries relying on these services, such as manufacturing, retailers, consumer staples, and consumer discretionary. Transportation also becomes more expensive. The domino effect extends to other industries. All these aspects weaken the purchasing power of consumers. This also increases the cost pressures of DPZ because higher inflation means higher prices of ingredients. Its delivery arm may incur more fuel expenses. In addition, it may have to raise wages so its employees can keep up with inflation.

Mitigants

Despite these headwinds, I believe that DPZ can insulate their impact.

One factor to consider is its delivery arm. Again, DPZ partners with delivery apps like DoorDash (DASH), Uber Eats (UBER), and Grab (GRAB) to strengthen its operations in two ways. These delivery apps ensure that DPZ stays on the radar. If customers search for pizza, the nearest DPZ branch will appear on the list. Instead of competing with these apps, DPZ utilizes them to strengthen or maintain its brand recognition. The story does not end here. Once the order is placed in these apps, they will be redirected to DPZ because its stores have their own delivery riders. With that, it only pays delivery apps for including or featuring them in all restaurant options. But the whole delivery fee goes to DPZ. DPZ and these delivery apps may also increase their delivery fees to offset the impact of higher fuel prices.

Another aspect to consider is its huge global market coverage. It already has over 20,000 stores globally, which ensures its solid brand recognition. But what is more important is that DPZ is franchise-heavy. If you look at its restaurant count, only 4% of its restaurants in the US are company-owned. The sale of its owned restaurants and their conversion to franchised restaurants not only resulted in cash inflows but also lowered its capital intensity and cost sensitivity. Focusing more on franchising makes expansion cheaper, asset-light, and low-cost. It also ensures a secure revenue stream. After all, its franchise fees are taken as a specific portion of sales of franchised restaurants, not their income. So, DPZ is sure to receive an amount from every franchised restaurant. And while I do not have actual data, I bet that no DPZ branch had zero sales. More interestingly, its franchising model also allows it to have multiple revenue streams. Aside from franchise fees, it also earns by advertising or marketing its franchised restaurants. It also earns by selling its own ingredients and supplies to its franchisees. Somehow, it's like monopolizing its overall business. It makes money by franchising and earns more from its franchisees.

Moreover, DPZ is very competitive. Because it has vast market coverage and sustained revenue growth, it can afford to offer deals to secure its customer base. Promos combined with strong brand recognition may lead to more demand. If I am to be honest, I have a bias towards DPZ because of its generous deals. If I can't access its website, I can order via Grab and even avail of its deals. The screenshot below proves that its deals extend to delivery apps.

Lastly, DPZ maintains a robust balance sheet. Its cash and cash equivalents nearly doubled to $233M in just a single quarter. This should not be surprising since it generated positive cash inflows. After all, its operating cash inflows remained high enough for its semi-asset-light business model with low capex. Its cash is also enough to cover all maturing borrowings and accounts payable. DPZ complements it with prudent debt management. It also maintains a long maturity profile since 99% of the total will not mature within a year. To be more specific, over 40% of its borrowings have a maturity of four or five years and longer. This is still in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of 4.5x. While its leverage is high, it still has enough capital buffer to sustain its operations and dividends.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update the Dividend Discount Model. The annualized dividend is now $7.96 based on the most recent quarterly payout of $1.99. So, the dividend growth rate is now 12.58%. Meanwhile, I increased its risk premium to 8.0% to price in more risks. Lastly, my estimated Beta is now 1.26, which means that DPZ has become less volatile than in previous coverage. With that, my new TP, or target price, is $418.95. The price drop just highlights the cheapness of the stock.

Meanwhile, its P/E shows that it only trades at 19.20x, which can be considered cheap for a standard fast-food restaurant. It's also much cheaper than Papa John's (PZZA), which already trades at a multiple of 41.12x. More interestingly, DPZ trades below the five-year average of 28.18x. If I use this to derive the fair value, the TP should be $489.43.

Technicals

DPZ's technicals are still bearish as the price decreases with lower highs and lows. It also trends below the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which means that the current momentum is still weaker than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. The 50-day SMA is also trending downwards, which means that recent market sentiments have been pessimistic.

Likewise, MACD and RSI show a downward trend. The MACD line is still decreasing, while the histogram is within negative territory, which implies that selling volume has outpaced the decreasing buying volume. However, RSI shows that DPZ is already oversold. This may open new buying opportunities since it also highlights that the selloff may be overdone, which is in line with valuation.

Conclusion

Domino's Pizza, Inc. remains resilient despite macroeconomic headwinds. This is supported by its strategic expansion, franchising model, solid brand recognition, and robust balance sheet. In addition, its valuation is already reasonably cheap with upside potential. Technicals adhere to it as bearish signals appear stretched due to overselling. I'm reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

data.title: Small-Cap Value Takes The Lead In A Risk-Off Quarter
data.description:
Value Wins the Quarter

The first quarter of 2026 ended with a decisive shift in equity leadership. The Russell 2000 Value Index gained 5.0 percent, while the Russell 2000 Growth Index fell 2.8 percent. The 7.8 percentage point spread represents the widest quarterly gap between the two style indexes since the post-pandemic reopening trade of late 2020.1

The pattern was consistent with historical precedent. Small-cap value typically outperforms in periods of elevated uncertainty and economic expansion, while growth tends to lead in earnings-starved or rapidly accelerating environments. The first quarter delivered uncertainty in abundance, with the Iran conflict, rising energy costs, sticky inflation, and tariff escalation all weighing on investor sentiment.2

The Russell 2000 overall gained 0.9 percent for the quarter, a modest positive return that nonetheless outperformed the Russell 1000 Index, which declined 4.2 percent. The mega-cap Russell Top 50 Index fell 7.9 percent. The quarter marked the fourth consecutive period in which small and micro-cap stocks beat their large-cap counterparts, solidifying a leadership cycle that began in April 2025.

Russell 2000 Value gained 5.0% in Q1 2026 while Growth fell 2.8%, the third consecutive quarter of value leadership. Source: Russell, Royce Investment Partners

Energy and Industrials Drive the Gains

Four of the eleven sectors in the Russell 2000 finished the first quarter in the black. Energy, industrials, and materials made the largest positive contributions. Oil, gas, and consumable fuels led at the industry level, followed by energy equipment and services and electrical equipment. The sectors reflect the direct beneficiaries of higher commodity prices and the domestic manufacturing boom supported by the One Big Beautiful Bill Act.3

The detractors were equally telling. Health care, information technology, and consumer discretionary weighed most heavily on the index. Software companies continued to struggle with the perception that artificial intelligence will eventually erode their business models. Health care equipment and supplies gave back gains after a strong fourth quarter as investors took profits.4

The sector composition of the Russell 2000 Value Index favors companies with tangible assets, pricing power, and domestic revenue exposure. In an environment where import costs are rising, energy prices remain elevated, and interest rates show no signs of declining, these characteristics carry a meaningful premium.

The Russell 2000 gained 0.9% in Q1, outperforming the Russell 1000 (-4.2%) and Russell Top 50 (-7.9%). Source: Russell Investments

Active Management Returns to Favor

The first quarter reinforced a pattern that active small-cap managers have observed for decades: periods of higher volatility tend to increase dispersion beneath the surface, creating opportunities for security selection to add value. Royce Investment Partners noted that active management has an “impressive track record” during periods of elevated volatility, as the gap between winners and losers widens substantially.5

The one-year returns ending March 31, 2026, underscored the breadth of the small-cap rally. The Russell Microcap Index gained 45.8 percent, the Russell 2000 advanced 25.7 percent, and the Russell 1000 rose 17.7 percent. The twelve-month spread between micro-cap and large-cap was the fourth-widest since the micro-cap index’s inception in 2000.6

The case for small-cap value rests on a combination of historically wide valuation discounts, accelerating earnings growth forecasts, and policy tailwinds from the 100 percent depreciation provisions in the Big Beautiful Bill Act. The risks remain substantial: floating-rate debt exposure, the maturity wall, and private credit stress could all create headwinds. For investors who can tolerate the volatility, however, the current environment presents what Royce described as “an especially opportune time to invest in select small caps for the long run.”

Active small-cap managers have historically outperformed in periods of higher volatility, as increased dispersion rewards security selection. Source: Royce Investment Partners, Franklin Templeton

Footnotes

1. Royce Investment Partners via Franklin Templeton, US Small-Caps Have Shown Their Resilience, April 8, 2026.

2. Franklin Templeton, US Small Caps Roll On Amid Increased Volatility, March 18, 2026.

3. Royce Investment Partners, Q1 2026 Small-Cap Sector Analysis, April 8, 2026.

4. Russell Investments, Russell 2000 Index factsheet, March 31, 2026.

5. Royce Investment Partners via Franklin Templeton, April 8, 2026.

6. Russell Investments, Quarterly Index Returns, March 31, 2026.

data.title: Ceasefire Without An Endgame: Tech Climbs The Wall Of Hormuz
data.description:
We’re back on a new Monday, albeit with the same old ceasefire in effect. Last week’s deadline for the U.S. and Iran to find common ground passed with no resolution. The deadlock we described a week ago in the “No Pressure Ceasefire” largely remains unchanged, with the U.S. and Iran not making it to another round of talks in Islamabad. While Trump had emphasized that last week’s deadline would see no extensions, we ultimately saw one emerge, with the explanation that it would give Iran’s “fractured” leadership time. Trump noted that:

Based on the fact that the Government of Iran is seriously fractured ... we have been asked to hold our Attack on the Country of Iran until such time as their leaders and representatives can come up with a unified proposal.

This time around, however, there is no deadline at all. Instead, the ceasefire is expected to last “until such time as their proposal is submitted and discussions are concluded, one way or the other.” Trump is holding talks with top security advisors this Monday on the Iran situation to move negotiations forward after Iran submitted a new deal, which Washington is rumored not to be too fond of. The Strait of Hormuz, which has been the most painful form of this war's global impact, continues to be closed by both sides: Iran has blocked the Strait, and the U.S. naval blockade remains imposed. Neither appears to be in any rush to reopen it before, at least, the other side retreats or a resolution is reached. Trump noted that:

When you have vast amounts of oil pouring through your system ... if for any reason this line is closed because you can't put it into containers or ships ... what happens is that the line explodes from within. ... They say they only have about three days before that happens.

So, we’re still in a ceasefire with a soaring market, led by tech, a closed Strait, and no clarity on the deadline of this now almost 60-day-old war.

An interesting phenomenon that surfaced over the last week amid all this is semis gaining huge momentum, with the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) crossing $10,000 for the first time ever (in fact, ending the prior week at around $9,555 and between two Fridays rising to $10,550), while software wanes for the most part, or at least in comparison. Since Q2 began, the outperforming league within our universe has been dominated by the semi industry. Last Thursday alone, semis outperformed software by an estimated 8.4%, and since April of last year, “the ratio of semiconductor stocks relative to software stocks has fallen -70 points, to the lowest in history.”

Movement on the Chip Wars?

Just within the past 24 hours, China’s National Development and Reform Commission blocked Meta Platforms, Inc.'s (META) acquisition of AI start-up Manus, noting that it will “prohibit foreign investment in Manus in accordance with laws and regulations and require the parties involved to withdraw the acquisition transaction.” This comes after China’s commerce ministry announced plans to investigate the acquisition at the start of the year. The NDRC's office is not acting in a vacuum; it is a visible response to the back-and-forth chip wars between the U.S. and China, in our opinion. While headlines are consumed with the louder U.S.-Iran war playing out, the U.S.-China chip wars are far from on pause. The House Foreign Affairs Committee, just a couple of days ago, cleared 20 new export control measures to tighten the chokehold on China’s access to advanced tech. Earlier this month, the U.S. introduced the MATCH Act, a draft bill that targets DUV tools sold by ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) and advanced etch tools sold by others. ASML took a steep hit on the news of the MATCH Act. We’re just recapping the past couple of weeks, but these fall into a longer tug-of-war between the U.S. and China in the tech race.

We’re circling back to Chip Wars now because a much-anticipated and pushed-out summit is approaching between Trump and China’s Xi in Beijing in mid-May. The move from the NDRC is telling of deeper internal politics and each nation’s fight to hold the global AI lead without giving the other any freebies. The background to Manus’ movements over the past year is also noteworthy at this juncture, because after raising $75M from Benchmark back in May of last year, Manus closed its China operations in July and moved to Singapore, where it could avoid the overhang of China's regulatory approval process. The staff has since even moved into Meta’s own offices in Singapore. The way we’re looking at the situation, this appears to be a warning of sorts from Chinese authorities to China’s AI start-up community, because what Manus and Meta may have pulled off sets a dangerous precedent that others could follow. Manus has been gaining more traction lately, as shown below, and as the AI race heats up, we believe China wants to safeguard its talent.

We’ve been waiting for the U.S.-China Summit since it was pushed from late March to early April and then to May. This meeting matters not only because it may ease the current geopolitical tensions around the Strait of Hormuz amid the ongoing ceasefire deadlock but also because it may be telling for CEO Jensen Huang’s efforts to bring Nvidia back to China.

The Analog Boom

In late March, we published a newsletter titled “Another Monday Madness: A Tech Take,” and within it, we pitched our answer to Where To Hide Out In Tech, and the answer was analog. We explained that:

When there are panics over price pressure, shortages, and longer lead times, everyone and their grandmother will pull in orders, or even double orders, to secure capacity at a more favorable price. Now, this happens across the supply chain, but the reason analog is special is that, unlike other sectors, analog tech doesn’t easily go out of fashion. Remember, we’re not talking about 5nm moving to 3nm; we’re talking about old generations. So, the backlash for holding more inventory than ideal on these chips if end demand doesn’t kick in is more manageable.

We saw this happen last April in the aftermath of Trump’s Liberation Day tariffs, and we strongly expected to see it happen again in reaction to rising supply chain risk as the Strait remains closed for longer. Likewise, we cited Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN) as our top pick for its diversified exposure across several end markets, which would bode well for our double-ordering thesis. We got the confirmation that we’re on the right track after TXN reported its Q1 '26 results after the bell last Wednesday, and the stock surged as much as 19% the day after. Management beat for Q1, as sales rose 9% Q/Q to $4.83B versus estimates of $4.53B. Here’s the breakdown of sales: analog was up 9% Q/Q to $3.92B, while embedded grew 9% sequentially as well to $723M. Industrial sales were up 20% Q/Q; auto sales were flat Q/Q; personal electronics were up 2% Q/Q; data center sales were up 25% Q/Q; and communications were up by about 30% Q/Q.

Outlook was the part we expected to be the booster, and management didn’t disappoint, guiding comfortably ahead of consensus for $5B to $5.40B against a consensus of $4.87B. The Q2 outlook is above typical seasonality, which management attributes to strength in the industrial and data center sectors but which we critically view as the result of pulls and double ordering. We expect this momentum to benefit TXN for one more quarter before letting up. TXN serves as a leading indicator for the analog peer group because it reports before the bunch, and we expect to see the momentum spill into others, including ON Semiconductor Corporation (ON) and Analog Devices, Inc. (ADI). We expect our analog thesis to hold for the rest of the peer group during this earnings season, particularly as the Strait remains closed, meaning supply chain anxieties have yet to subside.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: First Interstate BancSystem Is Getting Closer To An Upgrade
data.description:
Back in late November of last year, I wrote an article wherein I expressed my view that First Interstate BancSystem (FIBK) had been successful in showing improvement when it came to profitability and asset quality. Before that point, I had actually been bearish on the company, rating it a "Sell." And sure enough, during that time, the stock had underperformed the market, rising only 23.8% while the S&P 500 was up 29.4%. Since upgrading it, we have seen continued underperformance, but only by a small amount. Shares are up 4.1%, while the S&P 500 is up 6.2%. Given the time that has passed, I figured it would be a wise idea to dig in once again and see what is going on. What I found is an institution with high asset quality. Financial performance is improving nicely. But for now, I think that sticking with a "Hold" rating is the right choice.

Of course, the picture here should be considered fluid. That means that it can change as new data comes in. It just so happens that, before long, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. That is expected to occur after the market closes on April 29. Analysts do believe that revenue will contract year over year. But they expect profitability to rise. Considering how the company has performed over the last year, seeing a rise in net profits would not be shocking to me in the least bit. But at this time, that alone will not be enough to justify an upgrade.

It’s Not the Time to Bank on First Interstate BancSystem… Yet

Perhaps the best place to start when it comes to analyzing First Interstate BancSystem would be to dig into its balance sheet. For the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, the institution reported deposits of $22.09 billion. That happened to be a bit lower than the $23.02 billion that the company had a year earlier. Another thing that I don't appreciate when it comes to the institution is the fact that 36.2% of its deposits were classified as uninsured at the end of the year. This is a bit above the 30% maximum that I typically like to see. So on the whole, I would describe the deposit situation of the company as a net negative.

As deposits declined, the value of loans dropped also. They fell from $17.64 billion in 2024 to $15.01 billion in 2025. That drop came from multiple categories, including construction loans, commercial loans, and consumer loans. We also saw a decline in agricultural loans. In terms of overall exposure, most of the loan book for the company is still attached to the commercial real estate market. About 53.6% of all of its loans fell under this category. By comparison, the next largest area of exposure would be commercial loans at 15.5%, followed by the 13.9% that involves residential real estate.

It would be unwise for us to ignore other parts of the balance sheet as well. Our prospect ended 2025 with $7.74 billion of securities. For context, a year earlier, that number was $7.74 billion as well. So at least we haven't seen any change on that front. Cash and cash equivalents, meanwhile, went from $896.6 million up to $1.31 billion. And debt changed from $2.22 billion to only $631.4 million. This is a big improvement, if I do say so myself. Management effectively boosted cash while simultaneously reducing leverage. Obviously, that came at the cost of a smaller balance for loans. But the strategy for the company seems to be working.

I say this because, as the chart above illustrates, the net interest margin for the institution rose from 3.18% in the final quarter of 2024 to 3.36% at the same time in 2025. Yes, there was weakness when it came to loans and taxable securities. But tax-exempt securities became more profitable for the company. More importantly than this, however, is the fact that the cost of deposits improved year over year. This makes all the sense in the world when you consider that, as the Federal Reserve slashes interest rates, First Interstate BancSystem can immediately reduce how much it is paying depositors. By comparison, assets have a tendency to adapt more slowly. As an example, of the $15.21 billion worth of loans that were on its books at the end of 2025 that were not due one year out or further into the future, $7.90 billion, or approximately 52%, of its loans are fixed. That means that the rest is floating or variable rate in nature. And even some of those certainly have adjustment windows and interest rate floors.

In the chart above, you can see how the company performed for the final quarter of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024 and how it has performed over the last couple of years now. What strikes me as particularly promising here is the fact that, thanks to the changes in the composition of its balance sheet, net interest income for the business expanded. On top of this, we saw a rise in non-interest income too. To be fair, that was largely because of a $62.7 million gain from the sale of its Arizona and Kansas branches. If we were to exclude this from the equation, for instance, overall profitability would have certainly been lower.

In the chart above, you can see how the institution is currently priced in relation to earnings. I actually stripped out the one-time events when calculating this price-to-earnings multiple. When I do that, I get an adjusted price-to-earnings multiple of 13.8. That is unfortunately above the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. And it means that three of the five banks that I compared it to in that chart are more expensive than it is. In the chart below, the picture looks a little different. This involves the price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book multiples. On a price-to-book basis, it ended up being the cheapest of the group. And when it came to the price-to-tangible-book approach to analyzing it, only one of the five institutions was cheaper. So at least in this regard, First Interstate BancSystem is looking fine.

In the first chart below, you can see the return on assets for the institution. At 1.60%, it is certainly well above the 1% minimum that I look for. What's more, that also puts it above four of the five banks that I stacked it up against. In the subsequent chart below that, you can see the same type of analysis using return on equity. With a reading of 12.40%, our prospect is better off than four of the five banks that I compared it to.

It was really tempting to upgrade the stock after seeing this. But one thing that counteracted this, in my view, was the non-performing loans and assets data provided by the company. In the image below, you can see that there has been an upward trend when it comes to the share of assets and loans that are non-performing. These readings are all now above the 0.50% maximum that I typically like to see as well. Having said that, the picture could always change as new data becomes available. As I mentioned at the start of this article, management will soon be announcing results for the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year. These results should come out after the market closes on April 29th. The current expectation is for revenue to fall from $247 million to $242.5 million. However, earnings per share have been forecasted to rise from $0.49 to $0.59. That would take net profits up from $50.2 million to $60.3 million. So there is some hope that the picture could change enough to justify a more bullish outlook.

Takeaway

Based on all the data provided right now, I must say that I am getting closer to being bullish about First Interstate BancSystem. But I cannot bring myself to pull the trigger on an upgrade just yet. I need to see some further improvements, especially when it comes to the valuation of the company in relation to earnings. Until such time that this occurs, I believe that maintaining it as a "Hold" is the right choice.

data.title: The Ceasefire Trade Tests The Bond Market's Resolve
data.description:
A two-week ceasefire between the United States and Iran sent Brent crude plunging from $109 to $95 on April 8.

Oil Drops, Yields Hold

The announcement of a two-week ceasefire between the United States and Iran on April 8, 2026, sent energy markets into a sharp reversal. Brent crude fell from approximately $109 per barrel to $95 within hours. West Texas Intermediate crude dropped to a similar level, erasing roughly half of the war premium that had built up since the conflict began in late February.1

The ceasefire allows oil tankers to transit the Strait of Hormuz, which had seen traffic plunge to just six vessels per day in March, down from approximately 130 before the conflict. The United Nations Conference on Trade and Development documented the collapse in shipping activity, which had forced global energy supply chains into costly detours around the Cape of Good Hope.2

The bond market’s response was notably muted. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) held near 4.37 percent despite the oil price decline. Investors appear to recognize that the ceasefire is fragile and that the underlying forces driving yields higher extend well beyond the Middle East conflict. The question is not whether oil prices will decline further, but whether the structural pressures on the bond market have shifted.

Brent crude plunged from $109 to $95 on April 8 after the U.S.-Iran ceasefire announcement, erasing roughly half the war premium. Source: Reuters, ICE

Tariffs, Debt, and the Structural Overhang

The effective U.S. tariff rate stands at 11.8 percent as of April 8, 2026, the highest level since the early 1940s, according to the Yale Budget Lab. Section 122 tariffs, Section 232 metal tariffs, and pharmaceutical tariffs have combined to create a trade policy environment that is raising costs across the supply chain. The Budget Lab estimates that the current tariff structure will cost the average American household between $760 and $940 in higher prices.3

The fiscal backdrop compounds the pressure. The United States must roll over approximately $10 trillion in maturing Treasury debt before the end of the year, on top of a $2 trillion annual deficit. The combination of tariff-driven inflation, massive debt supply, and persistent conflict premium has created what one analyst described as “compound stress” that the bond market is pricing simultaneously.4

Investment-grade option-adjusted spreads remain at 89 basis points, wider than the 78 basis points at year-end. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index has lost 2.49 percent year-to-date. The “Sell America” trade narrative, which first surfaced during the tariff shocks of April 2025, has returned to investor discussions as foreign demand for Treasuries shows signs of cooling.

The effective U.S. tariff rate has risen to 11.8%, the highest level since the early 1940s. Source: Yale Budget Lab

What the Ceasefire Changes and What It Does Not

The ceasefire, if it holds, removes the most acute supply disruption from the oil market. Analysts at Lipow Oil Associates project that gas prices, currently averaging $4.16 per gallon nationally, could decline by 20 to 40 cents per gallon over the next two weeks. The pre-conflict national average was just below $3 per gallon.5

The ceasefire does not address the structural forces keeping yields elevated. Core PCE inflation remains above 3 percent. The Federal Reserve has signaled no rate cuts for the remainder of 2026. The tariff regime shows no signs of easing. Foreign central banks and sovereign wealth funds have reduced their Treasury holdings in recent months. The term premium demanded by investors to hold long-duration government debt has expanded to levels not seen since the 2022 tightening cycle.6

For fixed-income investors, the ceasefire represents a tactical opportunity, not a strategic inflection point. Oil-driven inflation pressures may ease modestly, but the underlying drivers of higher yields remain intact. The bond market’s muted reaction to the ceasefire tells the story clearly: this market is pricing risks that extend well beyond the Persian Gulf.

The U.S. must roll over approximately $10 trillion in maturing Treasury debt in 2026, compounding fiscal pressure on yields. Source: U.S. Treasury Department

Footnotes

1. CNBC, Gas Prices Should Soon Start Easing If Ceasefire Holds, April 8, 2026.

2. United Nations Conference on Trade and Development, Strait of Hormuz Shipping Data, March 2026.

3. Yale Budget Lab, State of U.S. Tariffs: April 8, 2026.

4. Wolf Street, 10-Year US Treasury Yield Flipflops Amid Massive Volatility, April 10, 2026.

5. Lipow Oil Associates via CNBC, April 8, 2026.

6. Federal Reserve, FOMC Statement, March 18, 2026.
